CHAMBER REVIEWS THE


PAST YEARS. WORK

pages.raw_text:

propose to put ourselves in the po-
sition of becoming a public
nuisance, either through smoke or

I'VE want to serve any or all of
these steam railways; We want to
be in position to be able to let
them into the city, We do not pro-
pose to kill our own business as
manufacturers of electric power
and operators of electric railroads
by letting them run through the
city with steam power, but they
might be unwilling to negotiate
with us under a franchise which
does not permit the use of steam
power. And l here announce to
this chamber of commerce that
this is our policy and intention"


D. G. Corbin, in responding to the
toast, Our New Years Present
The Spokane international" told
of the building of that line into
Spokane and how it had done much
to relieve the fuel situation in this
city. He said $4,000 tons of coal
had been brought in over that line
in the two months it has been op-
Grating.


Secretary Monroe reported the
chamber out of debt and balance
in the treasury of $S6S72.


DR.. O. w. Van Osdel spoke on
"Spokane's First Great Conven-
ton" He said that the new armory
should be constructed with seat
ing capacity of 5000 in order to
| accommodate the Baptist conven-


ton He estimated that each visitor


would spend s. per day while here


and pointed out the necessity for
ample accommodation and the de-
STABILITY of securing as large an
attendance as possible The first
| meeting Of the convention will be
held on July 4, and the speaker
suggested that this city invite the
| whole Inland empire to participate
and make patriotic observance Of


the services.
